summarization.a: Optimized molecular structure of methane (CH₄) used as a reference hydrocarbon precursor for SiC ALD.
summarization.b: Optimized molecular structure of ethane (C₂H₆), a simple hydrocarbon considered for ALD studies.
summarization.c: Optimized molecular structure of propane (C₃H₈), illustrating hydrocarbon geometry relevant to precursor evaluation.
summarization.d: Optimized molecular structure of butane (C₄H₁₀), included as a simple carbon precursor for SiC ALD.
summarization.e: Optimized molecular structure of pentane (C₅H₁₂), demonstrating hydrocarbon precursor geometry.
summarization.f: Optimized molecular structure of hexane (C₆H₁₄), representing extended-chain hydrocarbons for ALD modeling.
summarization.g: Optimized molecular structure of ethylene (C₂H₄), an alkene hydrocarbon evaluated as a precursor.
summarization.h: Optimized molecular structure of propylene (C₃H₆), showing double-bond geometry relevant for ALD.
summarization.i: Optimized molecular structure of 1-butyne (C₄H₆), an alkyne hydrocarbon considered for precursor reactivity.
summarization.j: Optimized molecular structure of 2-butyne (C₄H₆), illustrating triple-bond geometry for ALD modeling.
summarization.k: Optimized molecular structure of chloromethane (CH₃Cl), a halogenated carbon precursor.
summarization.l: Optimized molecular structure of dichloromethane (CH₂Cl₂), showing halogen substitution effects on ALD reactivity.
summarization.m: Optimized molecular structure of trichloromethane (CHCl₃), included to assess halogenated precursor behavior.
summarization.n: Optimized molecular structure of tetrachloromethane (CCl₄), a fully halogenated carbon precursor.
summarization.o: Optimized molecular structure of silane (SiH₄), a silicon hydride precursor for ALD.
summarization.p: Optimized molecular structure of disilane (Si₂H₆), showing extended silicon hydride geometry.
summarization.q: Optimized molecular structure of trisilane (Si₃H₈), included as a higher-order silicon hydride precursor.
summarization.r: Optimized molecular structure of chlorosilane (SiH₃Cl), illustrating halogenated silicon species for ALD.
summarization.s: Optimized molecular structure of dichlorosilane (SiH₂Cl₂), showing substitution effects on reactivity.
summarization.t: Optimized molecular structure of trichlorosilane (SiHCl₃), a silicon precursor with increased halogenation.
summarization.u: Optimized molecular structure of tetrachlorosilane, a silicon precursor with increased halogenation.
summarization.v: Optimized molecular structure of fluorosilane (SiH₃F), representing fluorine-substituted silicon precursors.
summarization.w: Optimized molecular structure of difluorosilane (SiH₂F₂), illustrating fluorinated silicon species for ALD.
summarization.x: Optimized molecular structure of trifluorosilane (SiHF₃), showing higher fluorination on silicon.
summarization.y: Optimized molecular structure of cyclic silane (Si₄H₈), representing ring-structured silicon precursors.
summarization.z: Optimized molecular structure of cyclic silane (Si₆H₁₂), showing larger ring geometries relevant for ALD.
summarization.aa: Optimized molecular structure of methyl-substituted silane (SiH₃CH₃), illustrating alkyl-functionalized silicon precursors.
summarization.bb: Optimized molecular structure of ethyl-substituted silane (SiH₃C₂H₅), showing extended alkyl substitution.
summarization.cc: Optimized molecular structure of amino-silicon precursor (SiH₃NH₂), highlighting nitrogen functionalization.
summarization.dd: Optimized molecular structure of alkoxy-silicon precursor (SiH₃OCH₃), illustrating oxygen-containing ligands.
summarization.ee: Optimized molecular structure of dimethyl-substituted silane (SiH₂(CH₃)₂), showing multiple alkyl groups on silicon.
summarization.ff: Optimized molecular structure of trimethylsilane (SiH(CH₃)₃), illustrating fully substituted alkyl silicon precursor for ALD.

d.question: Why is it important to model and compare a broad range of precursor molecular structures when screening candidates for ALD of SiC?
d.answer: Modeling a wide variety of precursor structures allows researchers to assess how different bonding environments, functional groups, and molecular sizes influence reaction energetics and thermodynamic feasibility for SiC formation. Since ALD relies on self-limiting surface reactions at relatively low temperatures, precursor chemistry plays a critical role in determining growth behavior. By comparing diverse molecules computationally, promising precursors can be identified efficiently before experimental validation, reducing trial-and-error and guiding the development of optimized ALD processes.

a.question: Were the molecular structures of the Si and C precursors optimized using first-principles DFT calculations before evaluating their suitability for ALD of SiC?

c.question: Which categories of precursor molecules are represented among the optimized structures in Figure 1?
c.answer: Saturated hydrocarbons (alkanes), Unsaturated hydrocarbons (alkenes and alkynes), Halogenated carbon compounds, Silicon hydrides, Chlorosilanes and fluorosilanes, Cyclic silicon-containing molecules, Alkyl-substituted silanes, Alkoxy- and amino-silicon precursors

b.question: What general trend distinguishes hydrocarbon precursors from halogenated and silicon-containing precursors in the set of molecules shown in Figure 1?
b.answer: Hydrocarbon precursors contain only C and H atoms, while halogenated and silicon-containing precursors include Si–X (X = H, Cl, F) or C–X bonds that introduce higher reactivity and different thermodynamic behavior relevant to ALD processes.
